reviews.review_text:
Buckshot Roulette is one of those games that looks simple at first, then suddenly you realize you’ve been locked in a psychological battle with a shotgun for 3 hours.

The atmosphere is insanely good. The lighting, the sound design, the creepy dealer sitting across from you everything feels tense even when barely anything is happening. Every round somehow turns into pure panic and mind games.

What surprised me most is how addictive it gets. You keep telling yourself “one more round” and then it’s 2 AM and you’re calculating shotgun probabilities like a scientist.

Such a weird concept but they absolutely nailed it.

In this duel to the death, you have a massive advantage by always moving first.  Yet it's still not easy to win more than 1 million consistently.  Just imagine how you would do in places like casinos, where you have a disadvantage.  In this sense, I would call it an anti-gambling game. 

Most achievements in the game are very easy to achieve.  The only two difficult achievements require accumulative winning to more than 1 million, which need some understanding about the game mechanism and a little bit of luck.

And never shoot yourself unless you are 100% certain it's a blank.

I picked up Buckshot Roulette recently on Steam for less than two bucks, and you get quite the experience for that price. The game features a unique concept that stands out from the mass of titles currently on the market. It’s highly engaging, short, and offers endless replayability. Overall, I had a great time with it.

[h1]Gameplay & Mechanics[/h1]
The core concept is a twist on traditional Russian roulette, swapping a revolver for a 12-gauge shotgun loaded with a randomized, hidden sequence of live and blank rounds. What really elevates the replayability, though, is the item system. Both you and your opponent—The Dealer—receive various items each round to turn the tide in your favor. The Dealer himself is a mystery; we don't know who or what he is, why we are playing this game of life and death, or whether he's a god or just a madman. His AI is quite impressive too—while he makes optimal moves most of the time, he can also make mistakes, leading to some surprising outplaying moments. The gameplay is fast-paced, incredibly tense, and naturally relies heavily on luck.

[h1]Atmosphere & Audio-Visual Style[/h1]
The game features a distinct retro, PS1-style aesthetic, meaning it can run on pretty much any PC. Its dark, gritty, and mysterious underground club setting feels daunting and claustrophobic, delivering plenty of creepy vibes. A standout detail is The Dealer himself, whose face becomes increasingly distorted the more you win against him. While there is no voice acting, the game communicates effectively through text and sound effects. The heavy blast of the shotgun can easily make you jump, and the sound of your heartbeat when you're down to your last charge really amps up the tension. No matter how far ahead you are, it always feels like high stakes.

[h1]Replayability & The "Addiction" Factor[/h1]
Buckshot Roulette is incredibly easy to pick up and play, and no two rounds feel exactly the same. It slightly reminds me of my Vampire Survivors days—while it's not quite on that exact level of obsession yet, I can easily see myself getting addicted to it if I keep playing. The "Double or Nothing" mode is where the real hook lies; just watching the score go up and barely surviving each round is a massive rush. There is plenty of content here for the right person. Honestly, it's a much better way to scratch that gambling itch than throwing money away at a real casino where you always lose anyway.

[h1]Final Thoughts[/h1]
For the price, this game is an absolute steal. I highly recommend it, even if you think the genre isn't for you or you've never played these types of adrenaline-fueled games. It is a genuine breath of fresh air from typical open-world or story-heavy titles, making it the perfect way to wind down after finishing a massive game.

[h1]Rating: 7.5/10[/h1]
dealer hot ngl, would give crazy teeth and even better backshots
Fun game with a simple concept. You can play it strategically, count shells and probabilities, OR you can just not pay attention and simply shoot for a surprise effect, both for you and your friends.

[h1]How gambling addictions start[/h1]

Buckshot Roulette is one of those games that somehow turns a few simple mechanics into an incredibly tense experience — borderline psychological warfare.

Each round forces you to make decisions with limited information. Managing items, tracking shells, and deciding whether to take a risk or play it safe creates a constant sense of tension. Even when the odds seem to be in your favour, the game always finds a way to keep you on edge.

While strategy plays a major role in determining the outcome, luck has a heavy influence on every match. Sometimes everything falls into place, and other times you're left wondering what exactly you did to deserve the shell currently heading in your direction.

In terms of achievements, there is plenty to work towards. Most of them are fairly straightforward, and you'll unlock them naturally through normal play. The ones tied to Double or Nothing can require careful planning and are heavily influenced by luck.

They will most likely leave you lying awake at 4 AM, desperately trying to remember how many live rounds and blanks were loaded into the shotgun.

Buckshot Roulette is simple, tense, and surprisingly addictive. It doesn't take long to understand, but every round feels different enough to keep you coming back for one more game.

[i]Turns out probability and I aren't on speaking terms.[/i] 8.5/10

[h3][b]One-line Review : Now, me and you, we are dancing on the edge of life and death.[/b][/h3]
[b]Rating: ★★★★☆[/b]

[b]Final Words[/b]: Reinterpreting Russian roulette in a fresh way, this is a simple yet entertaining game. At its core, the gameplay relies on luck, but the presence of various items allows you to strategically calculate your moves and tilt the odds in your favor, which is part of the charm. The overall game design is also excellent. Its distinctive visuals and simple yet repetitive music greatly enhance the atmosphere. Do you have the courage to point the gun at yourself for the sake of money, when the time comes?

Buckshot Roulette is a brutally simple idea that somehow turns into one of the most tense and stylish horror-roulette experiences you can play.
The concept is basically Russian roulette with a shotgun, but layered with items, mind games, and escalating risk that turns every round into a psychological battle as much as a game of chance.

What makes it work so well is the atmosphere. The dim industrial setting, the cold dealer, the sound design, and the constant silence between shots all create this uncomfortable tension where even small decisions feel huge. Every pull of the trigger feels like it might end everything instantly.

The item system adds just enough strategy to stop it from being pure luck. Being able to peek, skip, heal, or manipulate the chamber turns each round into a mix of prediction, bluffing, and desperation. When you win, it feels earned. When you lose, it feels personal.

The good:
- Extremely tense and atmospheric gameplay
- Simple rules that create deep psychological pressure
- Great sound design and presentation
- Item system adds smart layers of strategy
- Fast matches that stay engaging
- Strong sense of risk vs reward
- Every decision feels meaningful
- Unique and memorable concept
- Perfect for short intense sessions
- Incredible “one more run” energy

The bad:
- Very luck-dependent at times
- Limited content and replay variety
- Can feel repetitive after many runs
- RNG can completely decide outcomes
- Not much long-term progression
- Some runs end unfairly fast
- Gameplay is intentionally simple, which may not suit everyone

Overall, Buckshot Roulette is a short but unforgettable experience that thrives on tension and atmosphere. It’s simple, brutal, and addictive in a way that makes every round feel like a life-or-death gamble.

Now I’m sitting in a dark room at 3 AM trying to calculate probabilities like some divorced dude who lost custody of everything except his gambling addiction.
10/10 experience.
The shotgun somehow communicates better than most people I know.

I shot my friend 10 times in the face and then opend this game for a online round. You can drink beer so thats a extra piont and sometimes the Voices talk with you. Its a very good game thx for develop this Banger.

I got the platinum for Buckshot Roulette in around 14 hours, and despite its simple concept, the game managed to stay surprisingly tense and addictive throughout the entire experience. Turning a game of chance into something this stressful and strategic was an incredibly creative idea, especially with how quickly each round can shift from controlled to completely chaotic.

Going for the platinum added another layer to the experience. Some achievements required patience, good decision-making, and a bit of luck, which fit perfectly with the nature of the game itself. The atmosphere also deserves a lot of credit — the dark visuals, sound design, and overall presentation create a constant feeling of pressure that makes even small choices feel important.

Buckshot Roulette is a unique indie horror game that turns a simple game of Russian roulette into a tense battle of strategy and luck. The dark atmosphere, nice sound design, and constant suspense make every round exciting and unpredictable.

What really stands out is the mix of chance and tactical decision-making, which keeps the gameplay engaging and highly replayable. Despite its simple concept, the game offers a surprisingly deep and memorable experience.

A creative, intense, and highly entertaining game that's well worth playing.

I saw the game on Twitter when it came out but I was never really interested. A game about shooting yourself seemed quite weird to me. Now that I suffer from depression, it is quite interesting. The PS1 graphics look nice and models and animations are well done. This game was made by one person. The game has an addicting gameplay loop of risk taking and RNG. You need to predict if the shotgun is loaded with a live or blank round. There are a bunch of items that can help you. Your goal is to kill the dealer to earn money. It’s simple, it’s short and it’s fun.

100% Completion Review:

I absolutely recommend playing this game if you're a normal casual player. If you're like me and want to 100% the game . . . please don't get this game. Some of the achievements in this game are an absolute pain to get and not worth it. The game balancing and gameplay loop is terribly unfun when it comes to the more difficult achievements. When you get to a certain skill level, the game comes down to pure luck and becomes unbearable to play. Please, only play this game for some casual fun and nothing else.

There is a story mode that will walk you through the mechanics, but really the main gameplay loop here is the endless mode and multiplayer mode (MP was added in an update). This is a fun game to kill some time if you don't have enough time to dedicate to another game on a particular day.

Buckshot Roulette is a weird little game that honestly surprised me. If you're bored and looking for something short, intense, and different, it's actually pretty fun for a while. The whole concept is super unique — it's like Russian roulette but with shotguns, items, and a demonic dealer. The tension is real, and the "gambling without losing money" vibe is spot on. I really liked how simple but addictive the gameplay loop is at first.
That said, it does get pretty loopy and repetitive after a few runs. Once you figure out the patterns and strategies, the magic fades pretty quick and it starts feeling boring. I got about 10 hours out of it, but most of that was in the first couple of days. After that I haven't really gone back.
Overall it's a cool indie experience and worth trying (especially for the price). Great if you want something quick and thrilling, but don't expect it to keep you hooked for weeks. Solid little time-killer.

This game is great. Across the two main single player modes the variety of items allows for interesting, and intriguing gameplay. The dealer feels like a real player against you (even though he is sometimes merciless).
